desc: Barranquilla is Colombia's major Caribbean port city, known for Carnaval, river-meets-sea geography, and a lively urban culture scene rather than beach-resort style tourism.
hls: Best-known internationally for Carnaval season, which drives the city's strongest annual demand spike.
Warm temperatures year-round; rain risk and humidity rise meaningfully from roughly May-November.
Strong food and music culture with Caribbean Colombian influences across neighborhoods and festivals.
Good base for culture-first travelers who want city energy plus quick access to Santa Marta/Cartagena corridors.
Newer riverfront projects (Gran Malecón area) add safe evening walking and social spaces.
Works well as a 2-4 night stop, especially if timed around events or regional connections.

todo.name: Gran Malecón del Río walk
todo.desc: Do a sunset-to-evening walk along the Magdalena riverfront for views, food kiosks, and public art.

todo.name: Barrio El Prado architecture loop
todo.desc: Explore early-20th-century houses and leafy avenues in one of the city's classic heritage districts.

todo.name: Museo del Carnaval
todo.desc: Use 1-2 hours to understand Carnaval traditions, costumes, and the city's signature music-dance forms.

todo.name: Bocas de Ceniza excursion
todo.desc: Visit the mouth of the Magdalena River where it meets the Caribbean for a distinctive landscape outing.

todo.name: Caribbean food crawl
todo.desc: Build one meal block around costeño staples like arepas de huevo, fried fish, and coastal ceviche styles.

todo.name: Evening live music stop
todo.desc: Add a curated bar or venue stop for salsa/champeta/Caribbean sets in safer, well-rated zones.

wifi.note: Business hotels and modern cafés are usually stable; verify backup power/connectivity during heavy rain periods.

prac.fltNote: No regular UK nonstop; typical one-stop options route via Bogotá, Madrid, Panama City, or other regional hubs, with biggest pressure around Carnaval and Jul-Aug.
prac.currency: Colombian Peso (COP). Card usage is broad in malls/restaurants, but carry small cash for taxis and local vendors.
prac.lang: Spanish is the working language; English is present in higher-end hotels and guided services.
prac.visa: Colombia entry/length-of-stay rules depend on nationality; confirm requirements and onward-ticket expectations before flying.
alerts: Carnaval weeks can sell out central accommodation early and raise prices sharply.
Heat index can be intense midday—plan outdoor sightseeing in early/late windows.
Use trusted taxi or app-based rides, especially after dark.

sweet: Late December-March is typically the driest and easiest weather window, while shoulder months can improve hotel value outside major event periods.
